coredata.dc:title: Self-healable and self-adhesive hydrogel based gas sensor using carbon quantum dots embedded ZnMn2O4 platelets for ppb level sensing of toluene at room temperature

Nanda, Om Priya
Badhulika, Sushmee
coredata.dc:description: Self-healing and self-adhesive materials are some of the most promising smart materials that are being actively explored for sensing applications. In this work, we report a multiresponsive, self-adhesive, and self-healing hydrogel based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in zinc manganite (ZnMn2O4) for the detection of toluene at room temperature. CQDs are synthesized from stone apple shell via hydrothermal method and ZnMn2O4 is synthesized using a co-precipitation process. An optimized ratio of composite crosslinked by polyvinyl alcohol, boric acid, and acrylic acid-based hydrogel (CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG) is prepared using cyclic freezing-thawing method. Detailed morphological analysis reveals the even distribution of CQDs on the ZnMn2O4 platelets which are then encapsulated in the polymer matrix. The hydrogel shows green luminescence under 365 nm wavelength of UV light and photoluminescence (PL) properties in aqueous medium. The performance of ZnMn2O4, CQDs/ZnMn2O4, composite, and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG towards toluene sensing is investigated where CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG shows the best response. The CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG-based toluene sensor exhibits a wide dynamic range of 550 ppb to 50 ppm with fast response/recovery speed (26 s/36 s), and a low detection limit of ∼ 502 ppb. This hydrogel sensor also displays an excellent selectivity towards toluene when compared to other volatile organic compounds (VOCs). A comprehensive sensing mechanism is also discussed using band energy diagram. The improved toluene detection performance is attributed to the structural merits and synergetic effects including the formation of p-p heterojunction, high specific surface, and abundance of O2 functional groups (Oc% = 72.24 %). Further, this hydrogel shows outstanding mechanical properties in addition to self-adhesion and self-healing efficiency (within 5 min) thus establishing itself as a robust material for wearable applications.

head.ce:title: Self-healable and self-adhesive hydrogel based gas sensor using carbon quantum dots embedded ZnMn2O4 platelets for ppb level sensing of toluene at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-healing and self-adhesive materials are some of the most promising smart materials that are being actively explored for sensing applications. In this work, we report a multiresponsive, self-adhesive, and self-healing hydrogel based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in zinc manganite (ZnMn2O4) for the detection of toluene at room temperature. CQDs are synthesized from stone apple shell via hydrothermal method and ZnMn2O4 is synthesized using a co-precipitation process. An optimized ratio of composite crosslinked by polyvinyl alcohol, boric acid, and acrylic acid-based hydrogel (CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG) is prepared using cyclic freezing-thawing method. Detailed morphological analysis reveals the even distribution of CQDs on the ZnMn2O4 platelets which are then encapsulated in the polymer matrix. The hydrogel shows green luminescence under 365 nm wavelength of UV light and photoluminescence (PL) properties in aqueous medium. The performance of ZnMn2O4, CQDs/ZnMn2O4, composite, and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG towards toluene sensing is investigated where CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG shows the best response. The CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG-based toluene sensor exhibits a wide dynamic range of 550 ppb to 50 ppm with fast response/recovery speed (26 s/36 s), and a low detection limit of ∼ 502 ppb. This hydrogel sensor also displays an excellent selectivity towards toluene when compared to other volatile organic compounds (VOCs). A comprehensive sensing mechanism is also discussed using band energy diagram. The improved toluene detection performance is attributed to the structural merits and synergetic effects including the formation of p-p heterojunction, high specific surface, and abundance of O2 functional groups (Oc% = 72.24 %). Further, this hydrogel shows outstanding mechanical properties in addition to self-adhesion and self-healing efficiency (within 5 min) thus establishing itself as a robust material for wearable applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs), which are produced by rapid industrialization, combustion of fuels, and chemical use, are a major source of concern for environment, industrial safety, and health [1]. In particular, monitoring toluene (C7H8) levels is essential for medical diagnostics and indoor air quality monitoring. The World Health Organization (WHO) has classified toluene as 3rdcarcinogen [2]. The recommended concentration of toluene in indoor environments is currently below 70 ppb at 25 °C [3]. In the context of medical diagnosis, the increased toluene concentration in exhaled breath is supposed to be a biomarker for lung cancer [4]. It is reported that patients inhaled toluene concentrations (10–100 ppb) are significantly higher than those of healthy people [5]. Therefore, to minimize the health risks associated with toluene gas exposure, timely monitoring of toluene in both indoor and outdoor situations is crucial. Toluene analysis is conducted using a variety of conventional techniques, including colorimetric sensing, liquid chromatography, and ion chromatography [6]. However, most of these tools are costly, not portable, and need specific equipment to function. Therefore, a gas sensor that is flexible, stable, rapidly reactive, reusable, and has cheap production costs seems more suited for wearable applications.

So far, toluene has been detected using semiconductor metal oxide (SMO) gas sensors because to its advantages in real-time detection, low mass manufacturing costs, and compactness [7,8]. Metal oxides such as ZnO and MnO are widely reported for the detection of toluene. However, due to the complex detecting environment, it is still challenging for the SMO-based gas sensors to selectively detect toluene gas. Moreover, the need to operate at high temperatures (> 150 °C) also limits the use of SMO in practical applications [9,10].

On the other hand, zero-dimensional Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining a lot of attention because of their superior properties that arise from quantum size effects. In addition, these also possess tunable fluorescence emission, distinct optical properties, low toxicity, strong biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, and are easy to synthesize. CQDs generated from natural organics, generally termed as “green” CQDs, have significant potential as sensing agents as they are abundant in carbon sources [11]. These “green” CQDs show unique characteristics when used as fluorescence probes in chemical sensing applications. According to atomic-scale heterostructures, their intrinsic extinction coefficients, size (less than 10 nm), and shape-dependent electronic structure are chosen to modify on the surface of substrates to improve the properties of the material [12]. Furthermore, when they combine with other nanomaterial like SMOs, they form heterostructures. Consequently, combining CQDs with less explored metal oxides would be a new strategy for improving gas-sensing capabilities.

According to previous reports, there are SMOs that have been modified with CQDs for gas sensing applications [13,14]. However, they are not as flexible because of the usage of rigid substrates which makes them expensive due to complex fabrication procedures and they are not versatile. So, an approach would be to explore the combination of CQDs with SMOs to develop a flexible gas sensor. In this regard, conductive polymer hydrogels would be a good choice because of their standalone flexible nature, high electrical conductivity, low cost, chemical inertness, and high stability [15]. More importantly, compared to other conductive materials, the hydrogel's composition indicates high water content, swelling characteristics, biocompatibility, and a larger capacity to diffuse small molecules. Recently, hydrogel with good mechanical properties such as stretchability, self-adhesive, self-healing have extensively been explored [16,17]. Unfortunately, the majority of hydrogels have poor mechanical strength, which restricts their performance as they have a chance to damage during the application process. To address this issue, development of a sensor with a longer shelf-life, self-healing property, and deformability is crucial to restore the original structure after being damaged. Interestingly, no works related toself-healing hydrogel-based on CQDs included SMOs have been reported for gas sensing to date.

Herein, we have successfully fabricated a novel self-healable and self-adhesive hydrogel by crosslinking CQDs/ZnMn2O4 with PVA/boric acid/acrylamide for the detection of toluene. Thereafter the toluene gas-sensing properties of ZnMn2O4, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 , and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG sensors were systematically investigated. Compared to mostly reported toluene sensors, the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 HG sensor shows a wide range of concentration from 550 ppb to 50 ppm with a low detection limit of 502 ppb. Additionally, this sensor demonstrated good repeatability, outstanding stability, and high selectivity to other interfering gases. The charge transfer mechanism is also discussed in detail using energy band diagrams. Furthermore, the hydrogel's reversible interactions including dynamic covalent cross-linking and hydrogen bonds, allow it to self-heal efficiently without an external stimulus.

CQDs are synthesized using a hydrothermal route and the detailed synthesis procedure is discussed in section 2.2. in the supporting information (SI). The step-by-step synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 (a).
ZnMn2O4 is synthesized using a simple co-precipitation process as shown in Fig. 1(b). The detailed synthesis procedure is discussed in section 2.3. in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQD incorporated ZnMn2O4 composite (CQDs/ZnMn2O4) and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite has been followed by our recently published work [42]. The detailed synthesis is discussed in section 2.4. in SI. The schematic diagram for the synthesis of CQD/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel is demonstrated in Fig. 1(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZnMn2O4 and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 based device
ce:section.ce:para: A straightforward drop coating method was chosen to fabricate the device, wherein the ZnMn2O4 and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite was dropcasted on a typical 1 cm × 1 cm silica glass substrate. The detailed fabrication part is discussed in section 2.5. in SI.
The hydrogel dimension was cut into 1 cm × 1 cm and metal contacts were made to the corner of this device using Cu-wires.
The detailed set-up and gas sensing calculation are discussed in section 2.6. in SI.
The detailed instrumentation part is discussed in section 2.7. in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis by SEM and TEM
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the as-prepared samples in this work were evaluated by FESEM, TEM, HRTEM and SAED analysis. Fig. 2 (a) shows the low magnification TEM images of bare CQDs. Upon high magnification, as seen in Fig. 2(b), the bare CQDs exhibits regular and uniformly spherical particles with the hydrodynamic diameter of the particle size between 50 nm to 90 nm. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the as obtained ZnMn2O4 is composed of numerous randomly oriented irregular platelets. After magnification, it is found that these platelets are in hexagonal shapes with 300–500 nm in diameter (Fig. 2d). In Fig. 2(e), the platelets-shaped structure with approximately 300 nm diameter of ZnMn2O4 is further observed from the TEM analysis, agreeing with the SEM observations. Upon high magnification of TEM (Fig. 2f), it is revealed that CQDs (marked as red circle) are monodisperse and embedded within ZnMn2O4 platelets (marked as yellow rectangle). It is clearly seen from Fig. 2(g) and 2(h), that CQDs are embedded in platelets. The synthesized CQDs are well-distributed and primarily spherical dots, as shown in Fig. 2(i). The size of CQDs is between 2 nm to 6 nm in the composite, indicating that the size of the CQDs become reduced when embedded with ZnMn2O4 in the composite. CQDs are graphitic in nature, as evidenced by their 0.19 nm lattice spacing, which corresponds to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon in the HRTEM image (Fig. 2j) [18]. The SAED pattern on single ZnMn2O4 platelets comprises multiple rings (in Fig. 2k), indicating the polycrystalline structure of the composite. Therefore, the co-precipitation method yields well-crystallized ZnMn2O4 platelets. Moreover, elemental mapping of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 was also conducted to confirm the homogeneity of the composite. The uniform distribution of all the elements such as C, O, Zn, and Mn in the composite as seen in Fig. S6 (a-e) and EDS spectra (Fig. S6 (f)), confirming the successful formation of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite. As shown in Fig. 2(l), boric acid and acrylamide crystallization exhibits a porous and interconnected structure of the three-dimensional hydrogel. This interconnection is generated because of the formation of hydrogen bonding between acrylamide and boric acid [19]. It is seen that the hydrogel has few numbers pores with 10 μm to 20 μm in size. This dense structure resulting due to the increased freeze-thaw cycles, suggesting improved physical as well as chemical crosslinking in the hydrogel. Furthermore, the cross-sectional view of the hydrogel is also taken as shown in Fig. 2(m), revealing a similar type of three-dimensional interconnected porous structure.However, more number of pore-like structures were observed in the cross-section as compared to top surface with a small pore size of 5 μm to 10 μm. In order to know the effect of sensing on the morphology of the composite, TEM analysis of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite after sensing was also conducted. As shown in Fig. 2(o), an aggregation of CQDs particles with slight deformation in the morphology is observed along with large particle size (20 nm to 50 nm) of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical analysis by XRD, FTIR, and XPS spectra
ce:section.ce:para: XRD is used to analyze the crystal structure of the synthesized samples; ZnMn2O4, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite, and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel (Fig. 3 a). The sharp XRD peaks at 18.5°, 28.86°, 32.01°, 34.03°, 36.48°, 37.57°, 47.74°, 56.81°, 59.73°, 64.08°, 68.16°, 69.34° and 74.6° were consistent with the (101), (112), (200), (103), (211), (202), (220), (312), (321), (224), (400), (305) and (413) planes suggesting the formation of tetragonal ZnMn2O4, with space group of I41/amd [20]. Moreover, the lattice parameters evaluated as a = b = 5.7220 Å, c = 9.2360 Å, are well matched with the literature (JCPDS File No. 71–2499). Thus, for the synthesis of ZnMn2O4, the co-precipitation technique considered in this work is extremely promising. The XRD peaks at 19.8° and 29.42° indicate formation of CQDs, which is attributed to the (002) and 44.62° corresponds to (100) planes of graphite [21]. The peaks at 18.08°, 26.59°, 29.42°, 32.01°, 36.23°, 51.05°, 54.19°, 56.58°, 58.84°, 60.74°, and 64.74° confirms the successful synthesis of composite. Furthermore, the same XRD peak with slight shifting at 12.66°, 18.16°, 30.42°, 31.75°, 35.29°, 38.73°, 41.26°, 46.96°, 48.27°, 49.47°, 51.58°, 52.41°, 54.20°, and 57.39°, suggests the successful formation of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel.

FTIR spectra were used to display the functional groups of the composite, as shown in Fig. 3(b). Two peaks at 545 cm−1 and 641 cm−1 indicate the successful formation of ZnMn2O4 spinel. The peak at 1576 cm−1 results from stretching vibrations of C-N bonds in CQDs. The peaks at 1720 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C-O bond. The peaks at 2927 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1 denote C-H and C-O-C bonds. The bands at 3123 and 3414 cm−1 indicate the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of N-H bonds of acrylamide and boric acid. Moreover, it is noted that the shifting of peaks at 565 cm−1 and 655 cm−1 represents the successful formation of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite. In hydrogel, the strong peak at 1643 cm−1 corresponds to the distinctive vibration of C=N [22]. Besides that, a broad range of peaks from 3200-3600 cm−1 suggests –OH stretching vibration due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Broad absorption band at 3113 cm−1 confirms the presence of N-H stretching vibrations [23]. This indicates the presence of excess amino groups and hydroxyl groups in the polymer matrix which increase the hydrophilicity in the compound [24]. Notably, the absorption peaks in hydrogel that emerged at 848 cm−1 and 924 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of BH2 as well asB-O stretching (B(OH)4), respectively, confirming the crosslinking between the polymers.

XPS was used to further validate the chemical composition of the composite and its hydrogel. Fig. S1(a) and S1(b) show the XPS survey spectra of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel, respectively that consist all the elements such as C 1 s, Zn 2p, Mn 2p, and O 1 s elements. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of hydrogel shows four deconvoluted peaks at 282.11, 282.89, 283.88, and 286.25 eV whereas for composite slight shifted peaks at 282.73, and 284.68, 285.48 eV, and 287.67 is attributed to C-graphitic structure (C-C), C=C, C-O, and O-C=O of CQDs (Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d) [25]. Moreover, the Zn 2p XPS spectra of both hydrogel and composite (Fig. 3e and 3f) are deconvoluted into two main peaks at 1019.5 ± 0.5 eV and 1043.1 ± 0.5 eV, which are accredited to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 suggesting the presence of Zn2+ in ZnMn2O4 [26]. The Mn 2p XPS spectrum of the hydrogel and composite was separated into two spectra at 640 ± 0.5 eV and 651 ± 0.5 eV corresponding to Mn2+ and Mn3+ ions along with satellite peaks at 647.3 eV and 656.1 eV as shown in Fig. 3(g) and 3(h) [27]. The strong electrostatic interaction of the hydrogel further reinforced the formation of the heterojunction, as evidenced by the slight shift in Mn 2p towards lower energy. As shown in Fig. 3(i) and 3(j), O 1 s XPS spectrum of the hydrogel and composite is deconvoluted into four distinct peaks at 530.02, 531.09, 532.71, 536.38 eV and 528.1, 529.6, 530.52, and 533.49 eV, respectively, for lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies, and chemisorbed O2, respectively [28]. The surface-adsorbed oxygen peaks in CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel increase (72.24 %) as compared to the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite (43.34 %). This information indicates that increased number of chemisorbed oxygen in hydrogel can interact strongly with toluene molecules as compared to the composite samples during sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical analysis by UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectra
ce:section.ce:para: To check the optical properties of the synthesized CQDs, UV–vis spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy are recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), two peaks at 402 nm and 459 nm are observed in the UV–vis spectra of CQDs, suggesting that the absorption happens due to the π aromatic system, specifically for n-π* and π-π* transition of the C=O and conjugated C=C bonds as reported in previous works [29]. From the tauc plot analysis, the band gap of CQDs was calculated to be 1.85 eV. QDs are extremely soluble in water, as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b). This results in a highly distributed solution that is visible in natural light. When exposed to UV light, the aqueous solution of CQDs emits a bright green fluorescence. For ZnMn2O4, the absorption bands are observed in the range of 300 to 390 nm of λ as shown the in Fig. S2(a), where the band intensity is slightly demised for the CQDs/ ZnMn2O4 composite as shown in Fig. S2(b). The transfer of charge between cations and lattice oxygen could be the cause of this phenomenon [30]. The calculated band gap for ZnMn2O4 and CQDs/ ZnMn2O4 is found to be 2.49 eV and 2.09 eV, respectively. From Fig. 4(c), it is seen that CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite shows a dark brown colour in natural light because of the presence of ZnMn2O4 powder. However, in presence of UV light, the intensity of fluorescence is seems to be less as compared to bare CQDs which is due to the non-fluorescence nature of ZnMn2O4. For CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite, the absorption bands are observed at 275 nm and 350 nm of λ as shown the in Fig. S2(b). This decrease in absorbance may be the result of a decrease in the number of particles of QDs. Moreover, in order to check whether the CQDs/ ZnMn2O4 composite are successfully incorporated into the polymer matrix or not, fluorescent property of hydrogel is also checked as shown in Fig. 4(d). When exposed to UV light, a green fluorescence is also observed at the edge of the hydrogel. The size of CQDs and its composite as well as excitation energy-dependent photoluminescence characteristics have been thoroughly examined. Fig. 4(e) demonstrates the PL emission spectra (λem) exhibited by the carbon QDs where the intensity is varied with the excitation wavelength (λex). The emission peaks were blue shifted from around 581 nm to approximately 563 nm when the excitation wavelengths were changed from 380 to 480 nm [31]. This shift might be attributed to the presence of sp2 clusters and lower energy gap. The CQDs shows the highest intensity PL spectra when stimulated at 520 nm. As λex increases from 480 to 520 nm, the emission peak red shifted from 563 nm to 568 nm, resulting in a progressive reduction in intensity (Fig. 4f) which can be explained by the variation in size of CQDs. The excitation depending PL behavior of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite with variation in λem is shown in Fig. 4(g) and 4(h). The red shift of the absorption peaks at 402 nm and 459 nm in the fluorescence spectra of the CQD/ZnMn2O4 composite is due to aggregation-induced quenching. This aggregation occurs because of the interactions between CQDs and ZnMn2O4 particles, leading to changes in the electronic environment of the CQDs. As CQDs aggregate as seen in the TEM images in Fig. 2(d), their individual emission centers are come closer, facilitating non-radiative energy transfer between them. This process often results in fluorescence quenching and a red shift in the emission spectrum. The emission peak was red shifted when the λex was increased from 320 to 340 nm. The emission peak was shifted from 522 to 568 nm when the λex was increased further from 420 to 520 nm. The maximum intensity was observed at 480 nm, unlike in the case of CQDs, which is 520 nm. Moreover, the PL spectra of composite have been mapped using CIE coordinates (chromaticity diagrams), as shown in Fig. S5. It is evident from CIE colour mapping that the characteristic colours of the emission spectra were changed from yellow, light green, green when excitation wavelength is 420 nm. When it is increased to 520 nm, the colour is changed to light brown. Similarly, the excitation depending PL behavior of the hydrogel with variation in λem is shown in Fig. 4(j) and 4(k). The emission peak was blue shifted from 508 nm to 495 nm and then red shifted from 490 to 525 nm when the λex was increased from 320 nm to 340 nm, and then increased further from 360 to 460 nm. The decrease in fluorescence intensity after 380 nm of λex can be attributed to fluorescence quenching when zinc manganite is mixed with carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Here, the excited-state energy of CQDs is dissipated through non-radiative pathways, leading to reduced emission of light. Another reason is electron transfer quenching where, zinc manganite exhibits semiconductor properties with accessible conduction and valence bands, thus accept electrons from the CQDs, leading to quenching. The maximum intensity was observed at 460 nm, unlike in the case of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite which is 480 nm. In addition, bare CQDs, the CQD/ZnMn2O4 composite, and the hydrogel show emission spectra approximately in the same position at 420 nm at same excitation wavelength (365 nm), confirming consistent fluorescence behaviour. Moreover, the PL spectra of hydrogel have been mapped using CIE coordinates as seen in Fig. 4 (l) and S10. It is evident from CIE colour mapping that the characteristic colours of the emission spectra were changed from light blue to light green to bright green when excitation wavelength is 460 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical analysis for self-adhesive and self-healing properties
ce:section.ce:para:
To check the mechanical robustness of the hydrogel, stress–strain property, self-adhesive, and self-healing properties are investigated. Initially, hydrogels' ability to adhere to a variety of surfaces such as metal, wood, paper, nitrile gloves, tissues, etc. was investigated by adhesion tests. After that, the lap shear test method was used to quantitatively evaluate the adhesion strength. Between the two substrates, the hydrogel samples (20 mm × 20 mm × 1 mm) were fixed by pressing them for five minutes with 500 g of weight. After that, it is put on the universal material testing machine (INSTRON), and the two substrates are pulled at a pace of 10 mm per minute. The following formula in Eq. (1) was used to determine the hydrogel's adhesion strength (Fa) to the various substrates.
(1)
F a =
F
S
× 100 %
Where, S is the hydrogel's area of contact with the tested material and F is the maximal tensile force throughout the stretching procedure.

The hydrogel can stick to a variety of surfaces, including aluminum, and tissue paper as shown in Fig. 5 (a), and 5(b), respectively. Some more images showing the adhesiveness to stainless steel, alumina crucible, glass, and polymer are also displayed in supporting information as shown in Fig. S3(a), S3(b), S3(c), and S3(d), respectively. One proposed explanation for the hydrogel's strong adherence to several substrates is the noncovalent cross-linking, which includes electrostatic attraction and dynamic H-bonding [32]. A schematic diagram of the lap shear method of hydrogel with a real image for checking self-adhesiveness to various materials is shown in Fig. 5(i). A universal test machine was used to perform shear measurements in order to quantitatively assess the adhesive strength [43]. The quantitative results demonstrated that the synthesized hydrogel has extremely strong adherence to various substrates (Fig. 5j). The adhesion strength was determined by dividing the bonded area by the measured maximum strength. Hydrogel has adhesive strengths of above 0.05 KPa on a variety of materials, as shown in Fig. 5(k). On silk fabric substrate, it shows a maximum adhesive strength of 0.52 KPa. The hydrogel has extremely strong adhesive properties due to the synergistic influence of multiple factors. The schematic mechanism diagram of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel for self-adhesiveness towards different substrates is illustrated in Fig. 5(l). The addition of PVA provides the system a lot of hydroxyl groups that can form hydrogen bonds with a lot of different substrates such as glass, and PTFE [44], which significantly improve adhesion.The remaining carboxyl groups, amino groups, and phenolic hydroxyl groups of boric acid and acrylamide in the polymer chain can be reversibly adhere to a variety of solid substrates such as steel, and aluminium via hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction, and metal complexation. Therefore, the overall adhesion effect includes a multitude of physical and chemical interactions, such as hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interaction, and metal coordination that are formed between the hydrogel's functional groups and the substrates [45].

In order to check the self-healing behaviour, the hydrogel's conductivity is tested by using it as a conductor to connect a circuit with a LED indicator. Fig. 5(c) represents the original shape of the hydrogel without any strain. The freshly prepared hydrogels were cut by a knife as depicted in Fig. 5(d). As shown in Fig. 5(e), it is clearly visible that hydrogels were divided into two separate pieces. After cutting the hydrogel, the circuit transitioned from closed to open. After 1 min, the two dividedparts of the original hydrogels were brought in contact with each other. After 5 min of contact, the separated edges bound to each other and restored to form a single hydrogel without external stimuli, confirming the quickself-healing capabilities displayed in Fig. 5(f). Fig. 5(g) displays the close and front view of the self-healed hydrogel. Because of the self-healing properties, reconnection in the hydrogel resulted in the circuit returning to its closed condition due to the restored resistance. As a result, the LED indicator is illuminated. After that, the self-healing hydrogel is stretched strongly by hand without fracturing at the interface (Fig. 5h). These findings reveal that the hydrogels' 3D-network structure and mechanical strength have been regained. The self-recovery of the hydrogel is attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds and borate ions together stimulate the strong physical–chemical bonding interaction with acrylamide, making the hydrogel network closer to making it self-healable hydrogel [33]. This property is also checked by a dynamic resistance study which is a sign of healing process in a conductive hydrogel as shown in Fig. 5(m). Total eight consecutive cycles are performed in order to check its self-healing capability. The conductive channels inside the hydrogel are broken as the hydrogel changes from its baseline state to a short-circuit state, resulting in a sharp increase in the resistance. Afterward, when the hydrogel “heals” and re-establishes its conductive channels, the resistance quickly decreases and returns to its initial value. As shown in Fig. 5(n), the enlarged image of the dynamic resistance curve of the two consecutive cycles is displayed. The quick recovery in 1.3 s to 1.4 s confirms the hydrogel's rapid formation of the internal conductive network due to highly active self-healing ability. Moreover, to evaluate the compression testing, cylindrical samples measuring 14 mm in diameter and 20 mm in height were compressed using a texture analyzer at a rate of 1 mm/min. For all samples, the compressive tests were conducted at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 5(o), compressive stress-stain tests of bare CQDs hydrogel and bare ZnMn2O4 hydrogel were conducted. The elastic modulus of the cross-linked network of bare CQDs and ZnMn2O4 hydrogel increases as compared to CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel. This observation confirms that the composite hydrogel has less stiffness modulus compared to bare ones and acts as an effective self-healing material due to formation of chemical cross-linking interaction rather than physical cross-linking. In addition, very less significant changes in the elastic modulus of the cross-linked network hydrogel are observed as seen in Fig. 5(p) before and after self-healing process. Afterward, the tensile stress-strain test of the hydrogels before and after the self-healing experiment at different time intervals was also performed using a universal testing machine (INSTRON) as shown in Fig. S8(a). As shown in Fig. 5(q), the mechanical property test is used to quantify the self-healing analysis of the hydrogel. The original hydrogel shows maximum stress, and strain of 0.132 MPa, and 181 %, respectively. After 2 s of self-healing, the hydrogel shows a tensile stress of 0.05 MPa which is significantly lower as compared to original one. In comparison, after 10 min of self-healing, the hydrogel tends to recover completely to its initial state (tensile stress and strain of 0.13 MPa and 183 %, respectively). The toughness was used to calculate the self-healing rate as shown in Fig. S8(b). Two sets of samples were taken (the original and repaired hydrogels) to investigate the self-healing efficiency. At 10 s of self-healing, the toughness of self-healed hydrogel is very less with less healing efficiency (37.22 %). As the healing time reaches 2 min, the stress–strain curve of the self-healed HG shows very similar results to the original, achieving better toughness and healing efficiency (96.35 %), demonstrating the good self-healing property. Furthermore, rheological tests were performed to verify the viscoelastic property of the hydrogel as well as the existence of chemical and physical cross-linking inside the chain (Fig. S8(c)). A rheometer using Bohlin CVO Rheometer, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK, with a cone and plate geometry (CP 4/40, cone diameter of 40 mm with 4° angle) was used to conduct the rheological measurement. Before testing, the hydrogel was immediately placed to the rheometer's bottom plate. First, an amplitude strain sweep was performed at 26 °C using a dynamic strain scanning measurement within the range of 1–1000 % with a fixed constant frequency of 1 Hz. With increasing frequency (from 0.1 to 100 Hz), shear storage and shear loss moduli (G’ and G”, respectively) were measured. The hydrogels demonstrated a clear frequency-dependent rise in modulus and transitioned to the “sol” state (loss moduli G” > storage moduli G’) under high shear strains of 1 % and 100 %. The remarkable deformation recovery capabilities of the hydrogel are confirmed by the recovery test, which uses 0.1 and 100 % alternating values. These tests show that the storage moduli could quickly return to almost 100 % of their initial values (Fig. 5r). After self-healing for 24 h, the rheological test was again conducted as shown in Fig. 5(s). The self-healed hydrogel also exhibits a sol-gel transition point in its rheology curve, indicating its elastic nature. However, the shear loss modulus (G”) for self-healed hydrogel is found to decrease more sharply with time, suggesting the less toughness in the hydrogel structure. Additionally, the possibility of the formation of the hydrogel without boric acid is also checked as seen in Fig. S8(d) and its rheological performance is determined. The hydrogel can also be formed without adding boric acid but the viscoelastic property is reduced as shown in (Fig. S8(e)).In comparison to the original hydrogel, the hydrogel without boric acid has a significantly higher energy storage modulus (G′), indicating good toughness and stiffness. Further, a higher η value, as seen in Fig. S8(f), validates the structure's higher rigidity. After sensing, a distinguish characteristic in terms of elasticity has been observed as seen in Fig. S8(g). In 0.5 ppm of toluene, a significantly higher energy storage modulus (G′) is observed, suggesting more viscoelastic properties in the hydrogel than the original. Further, a much higher η value in 1 ppm of toluene confirms that the viscoelastic property of the hydrogel increases as the concentration of toluene increases. In addition, the swelling ratio of the hydrogel and water content is also noted. The gravimetric approach was used to measure the hydrogel's rate of swelling. Deionized water was used to immerse the hydrogel sample. The hydrogel was taken out and measured the weight at regular time intervals after removing the excess water using a filter paper.

The following formula was used to calculate the swelling ratio,
(2) Swelling ratio (Sr) = (Wt − W) /W.

Where, W is the weight of the original hydrogel sample and Wt is the weight of the hydrogel sample following water absorption at a specific time period. Fig. S7(a) shows the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel's swelling behaviour in deionized water. During the first 24 h of the soaking process, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel absorbed water in a faster way. After 40 h of immersion in water, the rate of water absorption of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel started to decline. Thereafter, after 72 h of immersion, the equilibrium of water absorption is achieved. Throughout the whole immersion procedure, the hydrogel is mainly swelled but not dissolve. The aforementioned findings demonstrated hydrogel's exceptional environmental tolerance as well as its significant anti-freezing properties. The equilibrium water content (Wc) of the hydrogel is calculated using the following Eq. (3),
(3)
W c
=
(
W wet
-
W dry
)
/
W wt
× 1 00

Where, Wwet is the weight of the fully swollen hydrogel and Wdry is the weight of the hydrogel in dry condition. The hydrogel sensor has more than 51 % water content, which can reach to equilibrium in a day. It is noted that sensors with high swelling ratio and hydrophilic environment are perfect for detecting hydrophilic gas molecules [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance analysis of ZnMn2O4, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite, and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensors towards toluene
ce:section.ce:para: After successful characterization, gas sensing performances of the sensors were evaluated. Firstly, current-voltage analysis (I-V) was conducted across a voltage range of -5 to + 5 V to investigate charge carrier mobility as electrical conductivity is an important factor for an appropriate sensor as shown in Fig. 6(a). CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor shows highest current followed by CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor and bare ZnMn2O4 sensor. The close-to-linear correlations between current and voltage of all the sensors suggest the formation of a low resistance Schottky diode, signifying the sensor's inherent ability to detect gas. After that, the I-V analysis of all the sensors was checked both in air and in presence of toluene gas as displayed in Fig. 6 (b). After being exposed to 5 ppm of toluene (reducing gas), a noticeable increase in current is observed, which clarifies the n-type behaviour of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor. On the other side, for CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor and bare ZnMn2O4 sensor, the decrease in current in presence of 5 ppm toluene suggests the p-type semiconducting behaviour of both the sensors. Following I-V tests, this sensor was subjected to a DC voltage of 5 V, and the resistance changes over time were recorded to evaluate the sensing performance. When bare ZnMn2O4 sensor was exposed to toluene, their resistance increased, and when the gas was taken out, it decreased as shown in Fig. 6(c). This behaviour again confirms the p-type semiconducting nature of ZnMn2O4. The time a sensor takes to reach 90 % of its maximum response value is known as response time, and the time it takes to reach 10 % of equilibrium of its base resistance value is known as recovery time. ZnMn2O4 shows a response value of 4.66 % in 20 s and takes 22 s for recovery towards 5 ppm of toluene, as shown in Fig. 6(d). Following that, current vs. time measurements of the composite sensor are performed. Similar to bare ZnMn2O4 sensor, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor also shows an increase in resistance when exposed to 5 ppm of toluene as shown in Fig. 6(e). This sensor shows an increased response value (8.3 %) in 18 s and recovery in 14 s towards 5 ppm of toluene, as shown in Fig. 6(f). After that, it was determined how the resistance of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel changed over time in response to 1.5 ppm of toluene (Fig. 6g). Fig. 6(h) illustrates the highest response of 5.1 % displayed by the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor to 1.5 ppm of toluene in 20 s. Additionally, the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel's gas sensing response to increased concentration (5 ppm) was evaluated (Fig. 6i). It was observed that CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel shows highest response of 13.32 % towards 5 ppm where response and recovery time increase to 26 s and 36 s, respectively with increase in gas concentration (Fig. 7 a). Hydrogel thickness plays a critical role in determining how quickly gas molecules diffuse through the material to reach the active sensing sites and influences the sensor's performance. As seen in Fig. 2(i), from the cross-sectional SEM image of hydrogel, the thickness of approximately 1 mm allows faster diffusion of gas molecules in the hydrogel as compared to bare and composite due to the shorter path length, thus, potentially reducing response and recovery times.

In order to verify the sensor's dynamic response, the hydrogel sensor's sensing performance was tested throughout a wide concentration range, from 550 ppb to 50 ppm. Fig. 7(b) shows that the sensor's response increases from 1.32 % to 402 % as the concentration increases from 550 ppb to 50 ppm. The CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor exhibits a linear correlation with concentration, as seen by Fig. 7(c) (R2 = 0.9868). The lower limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/s) can be measured by analyzing the response vs toluene concentration curve's slope. The device's sensitivity (S) is defined as the slope of the linear component of the calibration curve is found to be 5.39 % ppm−1 with a low LOD of 520 ppb, making it appropriate for real-time applications. As seen in Fig. 7(d), the hydrogel sensor's repeatability was assessed by exposing it continuously to 5 ppm of toluene for 7 cycles. In the cycle test, full recovery was attained when the toluene exposure duration was extended to 100 s. The sensor had a very consistent response of 13.32 ± 0.5 % for the entire experiment, with no noticeable response deprivation. As represented in Fig. S4(a), the response of the hydrogel sensor is tested in different weight ratios of (5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 15 wt%) PVA/boric acid/acrylamide with CQDs/ZnMn2O4. At room temperature, the hydrogel sensor responded highest to 5 ppm of toluene at a weight ratio of 10 wt%. A highly selective sensor is one that responds strongly to a single gas and does not interfere with other gases. As shown in Fig. 7(e), the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor was also subjected to a range of gases, such as xylene, ammonia, formaldehyde, isoprene, and benzene. A very little response of ZnMn2O4 sensor towards (dimethylamine, 6 ppm), (ammonia, 5 ppm), (formaldehyde, 8 ppm), (benzene, 5 ppm), (acetone, 5 ppm), suggesting that ZnMn2O4 sensor has a very limited adsorption capability for these gases. In comparison, CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor shows comparatively higher sensitivity to toluene along with other interfering gases (1.01 % to isoprene, 4.01 % to xylene, 3.05 % to ammonia, 1.37 % to formaldehyde and 2.08 % to benzene). However, the response of hydrogel sensor is significantly changed towards toluene, indicating the highest degree of selectivity. CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel shows response towards all gases with better selectivity (1.68 % to acetone, 7.01 % to dimethylamine, 6.23 % to ammonia, 2.01 % to formaldehyde and 3.98 % to benzene) towards 5 ppm of toluene gas.However, the sensing signal of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor towards 5 ppm of toluene has slightly interfered with its analogues in higher concentrations such as 6 ppm of dimethylamine and 5 ppm of benzene when compared to isoprene and formaldehyde. However, the responses of these sensors towards these two interfering gases (dimethylamine and benzene) were less than 7 %, suggesting highest degree of selectivity of the sensors. The hydrogel contains functional groups such as –OH and –COOH, which interact with VOCs through hydrogen bonding. The binding affinity of these groups varies based on the electronic and steric properties of the gas molecules. Toluene’s single methyl group allows for stronger and more specific interactions with the functional groups in hydrogel, while the additional methyl groups dimethylaminecreate steric hindrance and the absence of substituents in benzene results in weaker interactions. A histogram in Fig. S4(b) depicts the device's exceptional reproducibility with less than 2 % inaccuracy in response when three different devices are used. The stability and accuracy of the performance of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor were also assessed. The response did not change significantly over a 30-days period, as demonstrated by Fig. 7(f) because of hydrogen bonding. The higher current response of the CQD/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor towards toluene as compared to CQD/ZnMn2O4 composite is attributed to several synergistic factors. The hydrogel matrix provides a three-dimensional (3D) porous structure that increases the surface area and active sites available for interaction with toluene molecules. The interconnected network of the hydrogel also reduces the resistance for electron transfer, improving the current response of the sensor. Moreover, the hydrogel’s porous nature enhances the diffusion of toluene molecules into the sensing layer, enabling more efficient adsorption and interaction with the CQD/ZnMn2O4. In addition, the hydrogel increases the adsorption capacity for toluene due to functional groups on the surface (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amino groups), enhancing its accumulation at the sensor surface. Moreover, a quantitative link between water content in the hydrogel and its sensitivity to toluene is discussed. Toluene as a hydrophobic molecule interacts with the hydrogel and forms favourable environments, enhancing its retention near active sites. Swelling also expands the hydrogel’s network structure, increasing porosity and providing more pathways for the diffusion of toluene molecules which improves ionic conductivity, and analyte diffusion, enabling faster and more efficient interaction between toluene molecules and the active sensing sites of CQDs/ZnMn2O4. Fig. S7(b) shows a correlation between water content of the hydrogel in different time intervals and sensing performance towards toluene. The sensing performance of the hydrogel increases up to 36 % of water content. However, water content of hydrogel reaches 40 % which leads to over-swelling, thereby diluting the concentration of active sensing components (CQDs/ZnMn2O4) and reducing the sensor’s overall effectiveness. Furthermore, gas sensing tests of the self-healed hydrogel was performed. In terms of resistance-time analysis (r-t), no significant change is observed for the self-healed hydrogel sensor. When the self-healed hydrogel sensor was exposed to toluene, its resistance increased, and when the gas was taken out, it decreased as shown in Figs. S9(a). Moreover, the sensor also shows similar sensing performance towards 2 ppm of toluene with 7.2 % response as compared to original one (S% = 7.36 %) (Fig. S9(b)). However, the response time and recovery times (40 s and 75 s, respectively) are found to be higher as compared to original one (20 s and 31 s, respectively) is due to is due to the change in water content after self-healing because of network reconfiguration or partial recovery which can impact swelling and deswelling rate. Moreover, when exposed to external stimuli, the change in viscosity of the polymer slow down the movement of molecules or ions during sensing, thus, response and recovery times increased.

Fig. 8 (a) depicts the possible sensing process of the ZnMn2O4 sensor using a band diagram in air and toluenegas atmospheres, respectively. The explanation of the sensing mechanism in terms of oxygen adsorption modulating the space charge area of the ZnMn2O4 reveals that the sensor mechanism is surface-dependent. When exposed to air, the sensor absorbs oxygen by extracting electrons from the conduction band and converting them into surface chemisorbed ions, according to Eqs. (1) and (2). The extraction of e-s from the conduction band results in the space charge region, which increases the concentration of holes and reduces the sensor's resistance. However, as shown by Eqs. (3) and (4), when the gas sensor is exposed to the strong reducing agent toluene, the gas molecules are first chemisorbed on the sensor's surface and then react with the chemisorbed oxygen ions, and further increase the concentration of holes and decreasing the resistance of the sensor.
(1) O2 (gas) → O2 (ads)
(2) O2 (ads) + e- → O2 – (ads)
(3) C7H8 (gas) + e- → C7H8 (ads)
(4) C7H8 (ads) + O2 – (ads) → CO2 + H2O + 2e-

In comparison to the bare ZnMn2O4 sensor, the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor shows superior sensing performance. The usual outcome of p-type semiconductor behaviour of the composite is due to CQDs and ZnMn2O4, as both are the prevalent component in the structure. Fig. 8(b) shows the band energy diagram of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite. It describes how the changes in band structure affect sensing performance by calculating the band gap of both CQDs and ZnMn2O4 using UV–vis spectroscopy. Additionally, the Mott-Schottky plots of CQDs and ZnMn2O4 demonstrate the negative slopes, suggesting the presence of p-type semiconducting behaviour as shown in Fig. 7(g) and 7(h), respectively. Furthermore, the positive slopes of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite further support the formation of p-p-type of heterojunction (Fig. S4c). Based on the Mott-Schottky plots, the flat band potentials of CQDs and ZnMn2O4 are calculated as −0.72 eV and 0.52 eV vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. As the work function of CQDs (5.71 eV) is lower than ZnMn2O4 (5.8 eV), electrons move from the p-type CQDs to the p-type ZnMn2O4 until the equilibrium between the fermi levels of both materials is established. A p-p heterojunction is formed at their interface when the CQDs are incorporated into ZnMn2O4 platelets. The p-p heterojunction development is the main cause for the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 sensor's outstanding sensing capability. A Schottky barrier is formed, as a result band bending takes place, and both charge carriers move until the Fermi level achieves equilibrium. The main charge carriers for conductivity in this case are holes. When the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 sensor is exposed to air, the sensor absorbs oxygen by removing electrons from the conduction band and converting them into surface chemisorbed ions. After that, the ionized oxygen is adsorbed onto the surface of the CQDs and a hole accumulation layer is formed. Thereby, increasing the composite sensor's resistance and the major charge carriers (holes) in the ZnMn2O4 increase. When this sensor is exposed to toluene gas, the gas molecules are adsorbed onto the active sites of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor and react with the adsorbed chemisorbed oxygen, releasing electrons to the conduction band. Consequently, the concentration of holes decreases at the interface and the electrons are increased. Because of the formation of p-p heterojunction and Schottky barrier between CQDs and ZnMn2O4, the electrical conductivity of the composite-based sensor increases when doped with hole, which increases the resistance of the sensor upon the introduction of toluene gas. Toluene as a non-polar aromatic hydrocarbon can weakly interact with the hydrogel via π-π interactions and induce minor changes in the electron density on the surface of the composite. This affects the band bending at the junction, and reducing resistance. This unique property enables the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 composite sensor to detect toluene with significant changes in response as compared to bare ZnMn2O4, thus proving its superior performance. Hydrogel-based sensors, in contrast to semiconductors, rely on hydrogen bonding between the –CH3 groups of toluene and the B(OH)4- on borate, –OH groups on PVA, and O=C–NH2 on acrylamide polymer chains [34]. During absorption, the target gas diffuses into the hydrogel, increasing its conductivity. The findings indicate the greater sensitivity of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor is caused by a higher concentration of chemisorbed oxygen (73.1 %) and hydrogen bonding between the borate ion and acrylamide that forms during hydrogel formation. The material was able to absorb more gas molecules and form hydrogen bonds with the negative ends of polymer chains, thereby increase its performance. However, the gas sensing performance decreases if these active sites on the surface of the sensor are saturated, which results in a slower sensor response and reduce sensitivity to the toluene. The replacement of oxygen molecules from the surrounding air to the sensing surface is another rate-limiting step, which is caused in dynamic environments. In the hydrogel, hydrogen bonding interactions rely on the accessibility of functional groups (–OH, –COOH). At higher gas concentrations, crowding effects between gas molecules may hinder the formation of these bonds. Therefore, diffusion limitations in the hydrogel matrix can also arise and decrease the movement of molecules toward the deeper regions of the hydrogel. In order to confirm the conductivity of each sensor, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted as depicted in Fig. 7(i). EIS plot typically consists of two components: a semicircle in the higher frequency zone and a linear component in the lower frequency region. It is seen that the Nyquist plots of CQDs, ZnMn2O4 , and CQDs/ZnMn2O4 are similar in nature, with a semicircle in the higher frequency region. When compared to bareCQDs and ZnMn2O4, it is noticed that CQDs/ZnMn2O4 exhibit the smallest semi-circle curve and lowest charge transfer resistance (Rct), indicating the highest conductivity of the composite. Moreover, the larger slope at low frequency observed in the straight line of CQDs/ZnMn2O4 could be attributed to increased ion transfer rates. Table 1 shows a comparison of the performance of toluene sensors. The majority of the sensors listed are either rigid or use flexible substrates to produce flexible devices, such as polyimide, polyethylene terephthalate, and polydimethylsiloxane. Where achieving good performance demands complex procedures, such as thermal evaporation, sputtering, screen printing, and 3D printing, which are expensive. Moreover, this type of fabrication technique also requires high operating temperatures, and time consuming. On the other hand, the hydrogel-based sensor reported in this work shows distinct advantages in terms of flexibility, sensitivity, and environmental resilience, reinforcing its potential for versatile gas sensing applications. The mechanical properties of the as-synthesized hydrogel, including tensile or compression stress, toughness, adhesive, and rheology, have also been improved. In addition, the hydrogel's conductive properties for self-healing hydrogel were thoroughly examined and confirmed. The quick self-healing efficiency (less than 5 min) based on the boronic ester bonds and hydrogel bonding increases its efficiency by greater than 80 % in 90 s. Lastly, the sensing abilities of the hydrogel have been totally restored, as evidenced by the rheological repeatability curves remaining at the initial level. In order to regain its initial sensing performance and stability, a hydrogel with strong self-healing properties may rebuild the entire mechanical and electrical network that has been damaged by repeated impacts, indicating promising application prospects.

To sum up, we successfully fabricated a physical-chemical double crosslinked CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel-based toluene gas sensor. This hydrogel shows outstanding green luminescence under 365 nm wavelength and photoluminescence (PL) properties in aqueous medium. The sensor exhibits a wide linear range of detection with a low LOD (502 ppb) and rapid response/recovery times towards toluene at room temperature. The formation ofp-p heterojunction between ZnMn2O4 and CQDs, large specific surface contribute to the enhanced gas sensing response in the composite. Furthermore, the band energy diagram is used to explain the sensing mechanism by calculating the band gap from UV analysis. The results show that a larger concentration of chemisorbed oxygen (73.1 %) and hydrogen bonding between the borate ion and acrylamide that develops during hydrogel formation is responsible for the enhanced sensitivity of the CQDs/ZnMn2O4 hydrogel sensor. This hydrogel sensor also shows high selectivity for toluene when compared to other VOCs. Furthermore, the hydrogel exhibits self-adhesive and self-healing capabilities, making it a suitable choice for smart material-based sensors in wearable applications.
